Congratulations on the Series B! That's great news. Happy to help.

Thanks! So pilot helps companies automate compliance workflows — SOC 2, ISO 27001, that kind of thing. The new feature is automated evidence collection. Right now customers manually gather docs, logs, etc. We want integrations that auto-collect based on policies.
Got it. So pulling evidence from multiple sources at scale. How are you thinking about the retrieval architecture — real-time pull, batch, event-driven?

We're TypeScript and Postgres on the backend, Temporal for orchestration. I'm thinking Temporal workflows for the evidence collection — they're great for long-running, retry-heavy processes. Each workflow knows how to talk to its integration.
Temporal fits well. One thing to nail: credential management for the integrations. How are you thinking about that?

Credentials encrypted in Postgres with AWS Secrets Manager, rotated quarterly. At runtime, Temporal workers call a microservice behind a private network for decryption. Keeps secrets isolated from the main app.
Smart isolation. That's a solid approach.
Thanks. We lost a senior engineer to Stripe last week, so the team is lean. I'm trying to keep the design simple enough that everyone can own it without over-engineering.
Got it. That makes sense.
For MVP, I'm thinking GitHub, Okta, and AWS CloudTrail as the first integrations. Evidence: code diffs, access logs, audit logs, timestamps. Should cover most of our early customers.
Good scope. Before you lock the schema though — how are you versioning the connectors? You'll want to iterate without breaking existing workflows.

Immutable bundles with schema versions baked in. New API versions ship as new connector versions. I'll track it all in Linear — I prefer it for this kind of work. Team can see what's EOL and what's current.
That's solid. Forward and backward compatibility without migrations.
Right. Next I want to work out the data schema and query patterns so compliance teams can actually search the evidence efficiently.

sessions.topic: deciding architecture for pilot (compliance SaaS)'s next milestone

transcript.content: Hey, need your thoughts on something. We're in sprint planning for the next milestone and need to lock down the architecture. Series B just closed - huge relief - but we also lost Sarah to Stripe last week, which is a bummer. She was a core engineer on the backend. So we need to be smart about what we tackle over the next 6 weeks.
Congrats on closing Series B! That's huge. What's the main architectural piece you're trying to nail?

We want to scale up our workflow processing 10x by end of Q3. Current setup is TypeScript frontend, Postgres database, and Temporal for orchestration. The question is whether our approach will hold at that scale or if we need to rethink something fundamental.
That's a solid growth target. Tell me more about your bottlenecks - are you hitting limits on the database side, the Temporal cluster, or the application tier?

Postgres is definitely the constraint. We're hitting connection pooling limits under heavy load. Right now we're processing about 2k workflows per day and want to hit 20k by Q3. The audit log is the real killer - it grows fast and gets hammered for compliance reports. We're looking at our options for scaling that.
Audit logs at that scale are tricky. You're probably looking at partitioning, read replicas, or a separate read-optimized store. What does your data model look like?

Our main tables are workflows, tasks, and audit logs. We use pnpm for the monorepo setup, so we've got shared utilities between the API and orchestration layers - that's worked out really well. But the architecture assumes single-database which is starting to show cracks.
pnpm workspaces are great for that. For the audit log specifically, date-based partitioning could be a quick win. How often are you querying historical audit data?

Mostly for monthly and quarterly compliance reports. Also, full transparency - I've got Ben every other weekend, so my available cycles are a bit squeezed for the next few weeks. We need to be realistic about what the team can handle with Sarah gone.

That's important context. I'd suggest splitting this into phases: Phase 1 is audit log optimization (doesn't require major refactoring), Phase 2 is a denormalized read model, Phase 3 is horizontal scaling if needed. That way Phase 1 can run in parallel with other work.
I like that breakdown.
For the read model, you could populate it from Temporal events without touching the main schema. Keeps things clean and lets your compliance queries stay fast.
So for that read model - should we use Postgres materialized views or spin up something like Redis? I want to keep the stack simple and not add more operational overhead.
I'd go with Postgres materialized views. Fits your current stack, less to operate, and should handle the throughput fine.

That makes sense. I think we can execute that - pnpm workspaces make it easy to decouple the concerns anyway. Let me map this against Ben's schedule and get back with a real timeline, but this framework feels right for the team we have.
Sounds good. Once you have timeline, we can formalize the spec.
Perfect, thanks for thinking through this with me.

Series B closed last week, which is great, but losing Sarah to Stripe means we need to accelerate some roadmap decisions. Good call. Stepping away for a quick macchiato—be back in 5.

Looks like polling. Honestly, that might be the right call for us too—less infrastructure complexity. What stands out is how they display deltas. Every change shows before/after state, not just the action. We're showing raw events. Their approach feels way more intuitive for compliance teams trying to understand what actually changed.
That's solid. Before/after deltas would require state snapshots though. Should we spin that up as a separate ticket?

Definitely. So we adopt their delta approach for audit, keep our event sourcing elsewhere, and simplify to polling. I'm totally available to push this forward—no dietary restrictions or conflicts getting in the way—and Ben doesn't arrive until Friday, so I've got the full week blocked. The Series B runway makes this actually doable.
Makes sense. Should we document audit findings in Linear first, then move to policy engine?
Yeah, let me organize the audit piece in Linear with tags, then we can hit policy management. Want to have the full map ready before Thursday's team huddle.
Sounds good. I'll help structure everything once it's logged. Should move quick from there.

Perfect. I'll put together that checklist this afternoon.

transcript.content: Hey. Got a CI failure on main—Postgres migrations failing in CI but passing locally. Need to debug this.
I can help. CI/local differences are usually environmental. What's the actual error?
ENOENT trying to read the migration file. File exists in the repo but CI can't find it.

That's your problem. pnpm's node_modules layout breaks the relative path resolution. Let me fix it with `import.meta.url`.
Perfect. Just so you know, we track bugs in Linear.
Good to know.

Daily when ready. We've got good iteration speed.
